title: Permanent Marker on Upholstery
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; fiber + code checks before solvent
stainChemistry: Same chemistry as permanent marker on carpet: Sharpie-class marker is concentrated solvent-based dye in xylene/toluene/alcohol, with a resin binder that locks into fiber once the solvent evaporates. Upholstery adds two factors: (1) CODE TAG — S-only upholstery is solvent-cleaning lane but often doesn't tolerate the stronger solvents needed for Sharpie, and (2) CUSHION BATTING — if ink soaked deep, home chemistry can't reach foam. Also: IPA (91%) is the safest effective first solvent. Acetone is stronger but DESTROYS acetate, modacrylic, triacetate — and microfiber upholstery often contains those. Fiber check + code check are both non-negotiable. Black Sharpie is hardest; colors easier.
whyThisWorks: IPA on a cloth softens Sharpie resin and lifts dye onto a catch cloth. Tamping from top + outside-in prevents spreading. On upholstery, working a catch cloth UNDER the fabric where possible (zip-off cushion covers, loose cushions) transfers dye DOWN and out. Dawn emulsifies residual solvent + pigment on W/W-S codes; dry-buff on S-only. Pro escalation threshold is moderate because cushion batting soak is the ceiling.
defaultAssumption: Kid on couch with a Sharpie — extremely common. Fresh = real chance. Dried into a tight weave = harder. Batting soak (pressed hard for a while) = pro. Microfiber couch + colored Sharpie = fiber-test carefully; many microfiber fabrics contain acetate-sensitive fibers.

spottingProtocol.agent: Code + Fiber Check — Before Any Solvent
spottingProtocol.instruction: Upholstery code: W / W-S = full protocol. S-only = solvent only (no Dawn + water rinse). X = vacuum only, pro. Fiber test: dab 91% IPA on a white cloth, press on hidden seam 30 sec. Color transfer / fiber change = STOP, pro. Clean = proceed with IPA. Microfiber often contains acetate-sensitive fibers — extra cautious on the test.

If possible (zip-off cushion cover, loose seat cushion, reachable from behind), slip a white cotton catch cloth UNDER the fabric. Dissolved ink will transfer DOWN into the catch cloth rather than spreading sideways. For fixed upholstery without underside access, skip this step.

spottingProtocol.agent: 91% IPA on White Cloth — Tamp From Top
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply IPA to a white cloth (NEVER pour on upholstery). Press and tamp from the TOP. Flip cloth frequently as it picks up ink. Work outside-in. 3–5 passes typical for fresh Sharpie. Ventilate — flammable. No flames.

If IPA stalls on stubborn ink AND hidden-seam test was fully clean (no color / fiber / texture change), re-test acetone at the hidden seam. Clean = same technique as IPA. Any doubt, especially on microfiber = STOP and go pro — acetone destroys acetate / modacrylic / triacetate fibers.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dawn Damp Cloth (W / W-S) or Dry Buff (S)
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: W / W-S: Dawn in cool water on damp cloth, work with soft toothbrush, blot. S-only: dry-buff solvent residue with clean cloth instead, no water added. Damp cloth only, never pour.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan with window open several hours to vent solvent vapor. Inspect in daylight. Residual ink = repeat IPA passes.

steps: 1. Code tag + fiber hidden-seam test with 91% IPA.
3. IPA on white cloth, tamp from top, outside-in, 3–5 passes.
4. Graduate to acetone ONLY if fiber-tested, all doubts = stop.
5. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) or dry buff (S).
6. Weighted dry towel + fan with ventilation.
warnings: FIBER CHECK FIRST — acetone destroys acetate / modacrylic / triacetate
CODE CHECK — S-only = no water rinse
Never pour solvent on upholstery — tamp with cloth

homeSolutions.agent: Two Checks Before Any Solvent — Code + Fiber
homeSolutions.instruction: Before rubbing alcohol or anything else touches the couch: (1) FABRIC TEST — is it safe? Acetone destroys acetate, modacrylic, and triacetate fibers on contact, and those fibers are common in microfiber and suede-like upholstery. Dab 91% isopropyl alcohol (drugstore) on a hidden seam first — if it picks up carpet color, fiber, or changes texture, STOP and go pro. (2) CODE TAG — find the tag under a cushion. W / W-S = full protocol. S-only = solvent-only, limited options. X = vacuum only, pro. If the cushion cover zips off and is machine-washable, that's often the best path — get the ink off with IPA in place first, then wash the cover cold.

If possible (zip-off cushion cover, loose cushion), slip a clean white cotton cloth UNDERNEATH the fabric before applying solvent. As the ink dissolves, it transfers DOWN into the catch cloth rather than spreading sideways. For fixed upholstery without underside access, skip and work carefully from the top.

homeSolutions.agent: 91% Rubbing Alcohol on a White Rag — Never Poured
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply 91% isopropyl rubbing alcohol (drugstore — the higher % than standard 70%) to a clean white cloth. DO NOT pour it on the upholstery — pouring sinks ink + solvent into the cushion batting where you can't reach. Press and tamp from the TOP. Flip the rag as it picks up ink. Work from the OUTSIDE of the stain toward the CENTER (counter-intuitive but prevents spreading). 3–5 passes for fresh Sharpie. Open windows — flammable. No candles, no gas stove, no smoking.

homeSolutions.agent: If IPA Plateaus — Stop and Consider
homeSolutions.instruction: If IPA runs out of effect, acetone is stronger — BUT only safe if your hidden-seam test was COMPLETELY clean AND you're confident the fabric isn't microfiber / acetate / modacrylic. Re-test acetone at the hidden seam first. Any doubt = stop and call a pro. Pure acetone on microfiber upholstery can melt the fiber.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn Damp Cloth OR Dry Buff — Code-Dependent
homeSolutions.instruction: W / W-S code: work a drop of Dawn in cool water into a damp cloth, press on the spot 3–5 minutes, blot. S-only code: DO NOT add water. Dry-buff the solvent residue with a clean dry cloth instead. Damp cloth only, no pouring either way.

Stack a dry white towel on the spot, weigh with a book, 30 minutes. Point a fan at the area for several hours with windows open to vent solvent vapor. Don't close the room up while solvent is still evaporating. Inspect in daylight. Residual ink ghost = repeat steps 3–5.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for S-Only, Microfiber, Batting Soak
homeSolutions.instruction: Call a pro if: the code is S-only and IPA plateaus, the upholstery is microfiber / suede-like / silk / wool, the hidden-seam test showed fiber interaction, or the ink soaked into cushion batting. Tell them: "Permanent marker on [W / W-S / S / X] upholstery, fabric [microfiber / velvet / cotton / silk / unknown], age [fresh / dried], home-treated with 91% IPA tamped from top + [Dawn / dry buff]. No pouring. Fiber test was [clean / showed pickup]. [Residual ghost / batting saturation / fiber concern]."

steps: 1. FIBER CHECK + CODE CHECK — both non-negotiable.
2. Catch cloth UNDER if possible (zip-off cover).
3. 91% IPA on RAG, tamp from top, outside-in. NEVER pour.
4. Graduate to acetone ONLY if fiber-tested clean — otherwise stop.
5. Dawn damp cloth (W/W-S) OR dry buff (S-only).
6. Weighted dry towel + fan-dry with ventilation.
7. S-only plateau / microfiber / batting soak = pro.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the hidden-seam test damages upholstery, if code is S-only and IPA plateaus, if the upholstery is microfiber / silk / wool / leather, or if ink soaked into cushion batting.
warnings: FIBER CHECK FIRST — acetone destroys acetate / modacrylic / triacetate
Flammable solvent — ventilate, no flame, no smoking
Never wipe ink — blot straight down or tamp outside-in

neverDo: Never wipe — blot straight down or tamp
fiberSensitivities: W / W-S standard upholstery: tolerates IPA + Dawn
S-only: IPA only, dry buff — no water rinse
Microfiber: often acetate blend — fiber test critical
Acetate / modacrylic / triacetate: acetone destroys — pro

materialWarnings: Microfiber + acetone is a high-risk combo

escalation.when: Hidden-seam test damages fiber, code is S-only with IPA plateau, cushion batting shows ink soak-through, microfiber with solvent sensitivity, or acetone-free can't finish and acetone isn't fiber-safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Permanent marker on [W / W-S / S / X] upholstery, fabric [microfiber / velvet / cotton / silk / unknown], age [fresh / dried], home-treated with 91% IPA tamped from top + [Dawn damp cloth / dry buff]. No pouring. Fiber test [clean / pickup]. [Residual ghost / batting soak / fiber concern]."
escalation.specialistType: professional upholstery cleaner with solvent spotting + fiber ID

contextQuestions: Upholstery code — W / W-S / S / X?
Fabric — microfiber / velvet / woven cotton / silk / leather?
Does the cushion cover zip off and wash?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Microfiber is the subtle trap — often an acetate blend and acetone-sensitive. Always start with 91% IPA, acetone-free. Zip-off washable cushion covers are the easiest path. Dark Sharpie colors (black, red, purple) often leave a pigment ghost even after the film dissolves.
